items.q: What is the difference between a hurricane and a tornado?
items.correct: Hurricanes are huge (hundreds of km) tropical ocean storms that last days; tornadoes are small (tens of metres) intense rotating columns from thunderstorms over land, lasting minutes.
items.misconception: Hurricanes and tornadoes are basically the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A tornado is just a small hurricane.
items.why: They differ in scale, formation mechanism, location and duration. Hurricanes need warm sea; tornadoes need wind shear in supercell thunderstorms — entirely different processes.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary data in geography fieldwork?
items.correct: Primary data is collected first-hand by the researcher (surveys, measurements, observations); secondary data already exists and is found in sources (census data, maps, published reports).
items.misconception: Primary data is more important and secondary data is less reliable.
items.random_wrong: Primary data is collected in primary schools and secondary data in secondary schools.
items.why: It's about source, not quality. Secondary data (e.g. Census) is often more reliable than small primary surveys. Good fieldwork triangulates both — the distinction is who collected it, not how useful it is.

items.q: Why is an ageing population a challenge for HICs?
items.correct: Rising dependency ratio means fewer workers support more pensioners through tax, increasing pressure on pensions, healthcare and social care services.
items.misconception: Because old people stop contributing anything to society.
items.random_wrong: Because old people consume too much food.
items.why: The issue is structural — the ratio of workers to dependants, not the worth of older people. Students moralise where the geographical concern is about funding flows.

items.q: Which is read first in a grid reference?
items.why: It's Eastings. “Northings” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: A point within a square. “A whole country” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many digits in a 6-figure grid reference?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
